title: Meshulam's Conjecture on Ruzsa-Szemerédi Graphs
statement_text: There are no $(r,t)$-Ruzsa-Szemer\'edi graphs with both $rt = \Theta\!\left(\binom{n}{2}\right)$ and $r \geq n^{\Omega(1)}$.
context_text: Meshulam conjectured that dense Ruzsa-Szemerédi graphs cannot simultaneously have near-complete edge density and a polynomial-size matching. This was disproved in a strong form by Alon, Moitra, and Sudakov [1], who constructed $(r,t)$-Ruzsa-Szemerédi graphs on $n$ vertices with $r = n^{1-o(1)}$ and $rt = (1-o(1))\binom{n}{2}$, vastly improving an earlier construction of Birk, Linial, and Meshulam [4].
paper_contribution: Provides an explicit, algorithmic Number-On-the-Forehead (NOF) protocol yielding a strongly explicit and somewhat simpler version of the construction from [1] that disproves the conjecture, and extends the disproof to uniform hypergraphs via Theorem 1.2.
notes: The conjecture is stated in prose without a labelled theorem environment; attribution to Meshulam is explicit in the text. Conjecture is already known to be false; the paper supplies an algorithmic re-derivation of the disproof.
paper_title: Algorithmic Number On the Forehead Protocols Yielding Dense Ruzsa-Szemerédi Graphs and Hypergraphs

title: Erdős–Rothschild Problem on $h(n,c)$
statement_text: Determine or estimate $h(n,c)$, the maximum number such that any $n$-vertex graph with at least $cn^2$ edges in which every edge is contained in a triangle must contain an edge lying in at least $h(n,c)$ triangles.
context_text: Erdős and Rothschild asked to determine $h(n,c)$; see [5, 8, 9, 10]. Szemerédi observed that the triangle removal lemma implies $h(n,c)\to\infty$ for fixed $c>0$, and the improved quantitative removal lemma [12] gives a lower bound exponential in $\log^* n$. Fox and Loh [13] showed $h(n,c) \leq n^{O(1/\log\log n)}$ for any fixed $c < 1/4$, establishing $h(n,c)=n^{o(1)}$. The paper extends the question to uniform hypergraphs via $h_{k-1}(n,c)$.
paper_contribution: Provides algorithmic NOF protocols that recover and make strongly explicit the Fox–Loh upper bound $h(n,c)\leq n^{O(1/\log\log n)}$ for $c<1/4$, and proves its hypergraph generalization: for any fixed $c < d_k$ there exists $b>0$ such that $h_{k-1}(n,c) \leq n^{b/\log\log n}$ (Theorem 1.1).
notes: PDF source; $h(n,c)$ notation and surrounding inequalities are clear from context despite PDF extraction.
paper_title: Algorithmic Number On the Forehead Protocols Yielding Dense Ruzsa-Szemerédi Graphs and Hypergraphs
